title: Food Waste: A Growing Problem
summary: A narrator examines the scale of global food waste, its environmental and social consequences, and the practical steps that individuals, businesses, and governments can take to reduce it.
body: Every year, approximately one third of all the food produced in the world is lost or wasted. That is an astonishing figure when you consider that hundreds of millions of people around the globe do not have enough to eat. Food waste occurs at every stage of the supply chain, from farms and factories to supermarkets and private homes, and the problem is getting worse rather than better. On farms, a significant amount of produce is discarded before it even reaches the shops. Fruits and vegetables that are the wrong shape or size are often rejected by supermarkets because customers have come to expect perfectly uniform produce. These misshapen items are technically fine to eat, but they end up in compost heaps or landfill sites. This practice has been widely criticised by food campaigners, and some supermarkets have recently started selling imperfect produce at reduced prices. At the retail level, supermarkets contribute to waste by overstocking shelves and discarding items that are close to their sell-by date. Many consumers have also misunderstood the difference between best-before dates and use-by dates. A best-before date indicates when a food is at its best quality, but the food is often still safe to eat after that date. A use-by date, however, is a genuine safety indicator that should not be ignored. Confusion between the two leads to large quantities of perfectly edible food being thrown away. In households, the most commonly wasted foods are bread, vegetables, fruit, and dairy products. Many people buy more than they need because of promotions such as buy-one-get-one-free offers, and then fail to use everything before it spoils. Better meal planning and shopping lists can make a significant difference at the individual level. The environmental impact of food waste is also considerable. When food rots in landfill sites, it releases methane, a greenhouse gas that is far more potent than carbon dioxide over a short timeframe. If food waste were a country, it would be the third-largest emitter of greenhouse gases in the world, behind only China and the United States. This fact alone should be enough to motivate action at every level of society. Governments in several countries have introduced legislation to tackle the problem. France, for example, passed a law in 2016 banning supermarkets from throwing away unsold food and requiring them to donate it to charities or food banks instead. This approach has since been adopted or considered by a number of other nations. Reducing food waste requires effort from everyone involved in the food system. Producers need to adapt their practices, retailers must rethink their supply chains, and consumers need to shop more thoughtfully. If we can cut food waste by just fifty percent, the impact on both global hunger and the environment would be profound.

annotations.exampleEn: The disruption affected the entire supply chain from producer to consumer.
Millions of tonnes of food are discarded every year.

annotations.meaningVi: méo mó, không đúng hình dạng
annotations.exampleEn: Misshapen vegetables are often thrown away despite being perfectly edible.
She puts all vegetable peelings on the compost heap.

annotations.meaningVi: những người vận động về vấn đề thực phẩm
annotations.exampleEn: Food campaigners have called for stricter regulations on supermarket waste.

annotations.meaningVi: nhập hàng quá nhiều, chất đầy kệ hàng
annotations.exampleEn: Overstocking shelves leads to large amounts of unsold produce.

annotations.meaningVi: ngày hết hạn bán (trên nhãn hàng)
annotations.exampleEn: The shop removes products that have passed their sell-by date.

annotations.meaningVi: ngày tốt nhất trước khi dùng
annotations.exampleEn: Many people throw food away when it passes its best-before date unnecessarily.

annotations.meaningVi: ngày sử dụng trước, hạn dùng an toàn
annotations.exampleEn: Never eat meat or dairy products after the use-by date.

annotations.meaningVi: có thể ăn được, an toàn để ăn
annotations.exampleEn: Most of the food that is thrown away is still perfectly edible.
Methane released from landfill sites contributes significantly to climate change.
Reducing greenhouse gas emissions is essential to fighting climate change.

annotations.meaningVi: mạnh mẽ, có tác động lớn
annotations.exampleEn: Methane is a far more potent greenhouse gas than carbon dioxide.

annotations.meaningVi: luật pháp, văn bản pháp lý
annotations.exampleEn: New legislation was introduced to reduce packaging waste.

annotations.meaningVi: ngân hàng thực phẩm, nơi phân phát thức ăn miễn phí
annotations.exampleEn: Food banks rely on donations to feed vulnerable families.

annotations.meaningVi: sâu sắc, to lớn, đáng kể
annotations.exampleEn: The changes would have a profound effect on the environment.
Buy-one-get-one-free offers often lead people to buy more than they need.

grammarNotes.title: Present Perfect Passive — Describing actions without a specific agent
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Bài dùng thể bị động hiện tại hoàn thành để mô tả các hành động không cần xác định rõ người thực hiện. Ví dụ: 'This practice has been widely criticised', 'This approach has since been adopted or considered by a number of other nations.' Cấu trúc: have/has + been + past participle. Thể bị động được ưa dùng trong văn viết trang trọng và bài báo.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: The present perfect passive is used to describe actions where the agent is unknown, unimportant, or obvious from context. Examples: 'This practice has been widely criticised', 'This approach has since been adopted.' Structure: have/has + been + past participle. This construction is common in formal writing and journalism.

grammarNotes.title: Relative Clauses — Adding information about nouns
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Bài dùng mệnh đề quan hệ để bổ sung thông tin cho danh từ. Ví dụ: 'Fruits and vegetables that are the wrong shape or size are often rejected' (mệnh đề quan hệ xác định, không có dấu phẩy). 'A greenhouse gas that is far more potent than carbon dioxide' — đại từ quan hệ 'that' giới thiệu thêm chi tiết về danh từ đứng trước. Mệnh đề quan hệ xác định (defining) không có dấu phẩy và cần thiết để xác định danh từ.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Defining relative clauses give essential information about a noun. 'Fruits and vegetables that are the wrong shape or size' — the clause identifies which items are rejected. 'A greenhouse gas that is far more potent than carbon dioxide' — identifies which gas. Defining relative clauses use 'that' or 'which' and do not use commas.

translationVi:
Mỗi năm, khoảng một phần ba toàn bộ lượng thực phẩm được sản xuất trên thế giới bị mất hoặc lãng phí. Đó là một con số đáng kinh ngạc khi bạn biết rằng hàng trăm triệu người trên toàn cầu không có đủ thức ăn. Lãng phí thực phẩm xảy ra ở mọi giai đoạn của chuỗi cung ứng, từ nông trại và nhà máy đến siêu thị và hộ gia đình tư nhân, và vấn đề đang ngày càng nghiêm trọng hơn thay vì cải thiện.

Trên các nông trại, một lượng đáng kể nông sản bị loại bỏ trước khi đến được các cửa hàng. Trái cây và rau củ có hình dạng hoặc kích thước không đúng thường bị các siêu thị từ chối vì khách hàng đã quen với việc mua nông sản có hình dạng hoàn toàn đồng đều. Những mặt hàng méo mó này về mặt kỹ thuật vẫn ăn được, nhưng chúng lại kết thúc trong đống phân hữu cơ hoặc bãi chôn lấp. Thực hành này đã bị các nhà vận động thực phẩm chỉ trích rộng rãi, và một số siêu thị gần đây đã bắt đầu bán nông sản không hoàn hảo với giá giảm.

Ở cấp độ bán lẻ, các siêu thị góp phần lãng phí bằng cách nhập hàng quá nhiều và loại bỏ các mặt hàng gần đến hạn bán. Nhiều người tiêu dùng cũng hiểu nhầm sự khác biệt giữa ngày tốt nhất trước khi dùng và ngày hết hạn an toàn. Ngày tốt nhất trước khi dùng chỉ ra khi nào thực phẩm đạt chất lượng tốt nhất, nhưng thực phẩm thường vẫn an toàn để ăn sau ngày đó. Tuy nhiên, ngày hết hạn an toàn là chỉ số an toàn thực sự không nên bỏ qua. Sự nhầm lẫn giữa hai loại này dẫn đến việc một lượng lớn thực phẩm hoàn toàn có thể ăn được bị vứt bỏ.

Trong các hộ gia đình, các thực phẩm bị lãng phí nhiều nhất là bánh mì, rau, trái cây và các sản phẩm từ sữa. Nhiều người mua nhiều hơn nhu cầu vì các chương trình khuyến mãi như mua một tặng một, rồi không sử dụng hết trước khi hỏng. Lập kế hoạch bữa ăn tốt hơn và danh sách mua sắm có thể tạo ra sự khác biệt đáng kể ở cấp độ cá nhân.

Tác động môi trường của lãng phí thực phẩm cũng rất đáng kể. Khi thực phẩm phân hủy trong các bãi chôn lấp, nó thải ra khí mê-tan, một loại khí nhà kính mạnh hơn nhiều so với carbon dioxide trong khoảng thời gian ngắn. Nếu lãng phí thực phẩm là một quốc gia, nó sẽ là quốc gia phát thải khí nhà kính lớn thứ ba trên thế giới, chỉ đứng sau Trung Quốc và Hoa Kỳ. Sự thật này một mình đã đủ để thúc đẩy hành động ở mọi cấp độ của xã hội.

Chính phủ ở một số quốc gia đã ban hành luật để giải quyết vấn đề. Ví dụ, Pháp đã thông qua một đạo luật vào năm 2016 cấm các siêu thị vứt bỏ thực phẩm chưa bán được và yêu cầu họ quyên góp cho các tổ chức từ thiện hoặc ngân hàng thực phẩm. Cách tiếp cận này đã được một số quốc gia khác áp dụng hoặc xem xét.

Giảm lãng phí thực phẩm đòi hỏi nỗ lực từ tất cả mọi người trong hệ thống thực phẩm. Nhà sản xuất cần thích nghi với các phương pháp của họ, nhà bán lẻ phải suy nghĩ lại về chuỗi cung ứng của họ, và người tiêu dùng cần mua sắm chu đáo hơn. Nếu chúng ta có thể cắt giảm lãng phí thực phẩm chỉ năm mươi phần trăm, tác động đối với cả nạn đói toàn cầu và môi trường sẽ rất sâu sắc.

questions.prompt: What fraction of all food produced worldwide is lost or wasted each year?
questions.explanation: The narrator states 'approximately one third of all the food produced in the world is lost or wasted.'
questions.hint: The narrator gives this statistic in the very first sentence.

questions.prompt: Why do farms discard fruits and vegetables before they reach shops?
options: They are too expensive to transport
They have exceeded their use-by date
Supermarkets reject items that are the wrong shape or size
questions.explanation: The narrator explains that 'fruits and vegetables that are the wrong shape or size are often rejected by supermarkets.'
questions.hint: Customers' expectations about how produce looks play a key role.

questions.prompt: How have some supermarkets recently responded to the problem of misshapen produce?
options: They have started composting all unsold items
They have lobbied governments to change farming practices
They have begun selling imperfect produce at reduced prices
They have introduced stricter quality controls
questions.explanation: The narrator says 'some supermarkets have recently started selling imperfect produce at reduced prices.'
questions.hint: The word 'recently' is a clue that this is a newer development.

questions.prompt: What is the difference between a best-before date and a use-by date?
options: Best-before means the food must be eaten that day; use-by means it is at peak quality
Best-before indicates peak quality; use-by is a genuine safety date
They mean exactly the same thing
Best-before is for fresh food; use-by is for packaged food
questions.explanation: The narrator explains that a best-before date indicates quality, while a use-by date is 'a genuine safety indicator.'
questions.hint: One date is about quality; the other is about safety.

questions.prompt: Which foods are most commonly wasted in households?
options: Tinned goods, frozen food, and snacks
Bread, vegetables, fruit, and dairy products
questions.explanation: The narrator lists 'bread, vegetables, fruit, and dairy products' as the most commonly wasted foods in households.
questions.hint: These are all fresh or perishable items.

questions.prompt: Why do many people buy more food than they need?
options: They do not check what they already have at home
They are attracted by promotions such as buy-one-get-one-free offers
They prefer to cook large portions and freeze leftovers
They are afraid of food shortages
questions.explanation: The narrator mentions 'promotions such as buy-one-get-one-free offers' as a reason people overbuy.
questions.hint: A type of supermarket promotion is specifically mentioned.

questions.prompt: What harmful gas is released when food rots in landfill sites?
questions.explanation: The narrator says 'When food rots in landfill sites, it releases methane, a greenhouse gas.'
questions.hint: The narrator also describes it as more potent than carbon dioxide.

questions.prompt: If food waste were a country, where would it rank globally for greenhouse gas emissions?
questions.explanation: The narrator states 'it would be the third-largest emitter of greenhouse gases in the world, behind only China and the United States.'
questions.hint: The narrator names the two countries that would rank above it.

questions.prompt: What law did France pass in 2016 regarding supermarket food waste?
options: Supermarkets must compost all unsold food
Supermarkets are banned from throwing away unsold food and must donate it
Supermarkets must reduce prices of food near its sell-by date
Supermarkets must report their food waste levels to the government
questions.explanation: The narrator describes the law as 'banning supermarkets from throwing away unsold food and requiring them to donate it to charities or food banks.'
questions.hint: The law involves a ban and an obligation to donate.

questions.prompt: What practical suggestion does the narrator give for reducing household food waste?
options: Cook all food immediately after buying it
Use better meal planning and shopping lists
Only buy food with the latest sell-by dates
Shop at farmers' markets instead of supermarkets
questions.explanation: The narrator says 'Better meal planning and shopping lists can make a significant difference at the individual level.'
questions.hint: This suggestion involves planning before you go shopping.

questions.prompt: According to the narrator, what would be the result of cutting food waste by fifty percent?
options: It would save consumers money but have little environmental impact
It would have a profound impact on both global hunger and the environment
It would solve the problem of greenhouse gas emissions entirely
It would mainly benefit farmers and retailers
questions.explanation: The narrator concludes that cutting waste by fifty percent would have a 'profound' impact on 'both global hunger and the environment.'
questions.hint: The narrator uses the word 'profound' to describe the scale of this impact.

cloze.template: Every year, approximately one third of all the food produced in the world is lost or {{b1}}. That is an astonishing figure when you consider that hundreds of millions of people around the globe do not have enough to eat. Food waste occurs at every stage of the {{b2}}, from farms and factories to supermarkets and private homes, and the problem is getting worse rather than better. On farms, a significant amount of produce is {{b3}} before it even reaches the shops. Fruits and vegetables that are the wrong shape or size are often rejected by supermarkets because customers have come to expect perfectly {{b4}} produce. These misshapen items are technically fine to eat, but they end up in compost heaps or landfill sites. At the retail level, supermarkets contribute to waste by overstocking shelves and discarding items that are close to their {{b5}}. Many consumers have also misunderstood the difference between best-before dates and use-by dates. A best-before date indicates when a food is at its best quality, but the food is often still safe to eat after that date. A use-by date, however, is a genuine safety {{b6}} that should not be ignored. Confusion between the two leads to large quantities of perfectly {{b7}} food being thrown away. In households, the most commonly wasted foods are bread, vegetables, fruit, and dairy products. Many people buy more than they need because of promotions such as buy-one-get-one-free offers, and then fail to use everything before it {{b8}}. The environmental impact of food waste is also considerable. When food rots in landfill sites, it releases {{b9}}, a greenhouse gas that is far more potent than carbon dioxide over a short timeframe. Governments in several countries have introduced {{b10}} to tackle the problem. France, for example, passed a law in 2016 banning supermarkets from throwing away unsold food and requiring them to donate it to charities or food banks instead.

blanks.explanation: The passage uses 'lost or wasted' — 'wasted' means used poorly or thrown away unnecessarily.
'Supply chain' is the specific term used to describe the entire journey from production to consumption.
'Discarded' means thrown away or rejected — the exact verb used in the passage for farm-level waste.
'Uniform' means all the same shape and size — the passage says customers expect 'perfectly uniform produce.'
The passage specifically says supermarkets discard items 'close to their sell-by date.'
The passage describes a use-by date as 'a genuine safety indicator' — a word meaning something that shows or signals information.
'Edible' means fit to be eaten — the passage says 'perfectly edible food being thrown away.'
'Spoils' is the verb used for food going bad — 'fail to use everything before it spoils.'
The passage states that rotting food in landfill 'releases methane, a greenhouse gas.'
'Legislation' refers to formal laws passed by government — the passage says governments have 'introduced legislation to tackle the problem.'

criticalThinkingQuestion: The narrator argues that reducing food waste by fifty percent would have a profound effect on global hunger and the environment. Who do you think bears the most responsibility for solving the food waste problem — individuals, businesses, or governments? Is legislation like France's the most effective solution, or are there better approaches?
